access.accessMethod: Read the checked-out CME submodule path
access.entityLabel: General Corpus source XML for acw2946

entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for acw2946
notes: The file is 149,658 bytes and has root element DLPSTEXTCLASS.

Against the 149,474-byte XML in the archived U-M package, the repository version adds an XML declaration, NODE identifiers, NOTE1/@ID, and serialization changes, and changes EDITORIALDECL/@N from 1 to 4.

rights.basis: The work was composed and first transmitted centuries before modern copyright; U-M also labels this item public domain and No Copyright–United States.
rights.component: Underlying anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English text
notes: This does not determine rights in modern editorial readings, OCR/markup, manuscript photography, or interfaces.

rights.basis: The current U-M item says the materials are in the public domain and applies DPLA No Copyright–United States.

entities.description: A public, uncorrected OCR text with Level 1 TEI-in-Libraries structural markup and links to page images of Triggs's 1895 edition.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item ACW2946.0001.001
notes: The item-specific declaration states that OCR was not corrected, content was not edited, and encoding followed Level 1 of the TEI in Libraries Guidelines.

The text layer transcribes the title page, dedication, twenty-seven-name interpretatio, and poem on printed pp. 1–61. Most of the lxxvi-page introduction and pp. 62–116 notes, index, and glossary are represented only by PB milestones in omitted-matter divisions.
The 196 page milestones correspond to all page images in the U-M source package, so text-layer selection and image-layer completeness must not be conflated.

rights.basis: The current U-M item says the materials are in the public domain and applies DPLA No Copyright–United States.

notes: No Copyright–United States is jurisdiction-specific and does not itself dedicate the markup worldwide.

rights.basis: The embedded XML says 'Where applicable, subject to copyright' and that other restrictions on distribution may apply.
rights.component: Legacy 2006 XML availability statement and distribution
notes: The relationship between the broad legacy warning and the current NoC-US item statement has not been clarified by U-M.

entities.label: U-M 196-TIFF page-image set for ACW2946.0001.001

physicalDescription.format: 600 dpi, one-bit TIFF G4 page images plus XML, text, header, pageview data, and ingest log
notes: Images are named 00000001.tif through 00000196.tif.
pageview.dat maps image 81 to printed page 1 and image 196 to printed page 116.

bibliographic.citation: Oscar Lovell Triggs, ed., The Assembly of Gods: Or, The Accord of Reason and Sensuality in the Fear of Death, by John Lydgate (Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1895), University of Chicago English Studies 1, lxxvi, 116 pp.
bibliographic.series: The University of Chicago English Studies 1; issued 1896 as EETS Extra Series 69
bibliographic.title: The Assembly of Gods: Or, The Accord of Reason and Sensuality in the Fear of Death

entities.description: The immediate print source of the U-M encoding, originally issued as Triggs's University of Chicago doctoral dissertation and reissued in 1896 as EETS Extra Series 69.
entities.label: Triggs 1895 edition of The Assembly of Gods
notes: Triggs calls Trinity R.3.19 the earliest and only authoritative manuscript known to him and follows it with a short explicit list of emendations.
His introduction prints a full collation of Royal 18 D II against Trinity and marks selected readings from early print Text D.

Chance reports that Fletcher found more than one hundred transcription errors and inadequate variants; the edition remains the source of acw2946 but is not treated as a diplomatic manuscript transcription.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1895; U-M asserts public domain/No Copyright–United States, Internet Archive reports no visible notice, and Copyright Office Circular 15A states that U.S.-published works before 1931 are public domain.
rights.component: 1895 edition text, introduction, notes, glossary, and typographic arrangement

access.format: On-demand photography; quote depends on item and use

rights.basis: Trinity's Wren Digital Collections page says viewer images may be downloaded for personal study but asks researchers to apply to the Library for permission to publish them.
rights.component: Publication and licensed reuse of Trinity collection images
notes: This general policy appears stricter than the exact object's CC BY-NC license and therefore remains a provider-interpretation question.

dateStatements.label: Fletcher/Chance date for the Assembly context
dateStatements.notes: The narrower date is scholarly and should not be substituted for the broader institutional date of the whole composite codex.

entities.description: A composite paper miscellany of Middle English verse and one prose item, principally late fifteenth century with later additions. The interpretatio is at fol. 67v and The Assembly of Gods at fols. 68r–97v; this is Triggs's copytext and Chance's authoritative Text A.

holding.notes: The codex belonged to John Stow and was given to Trinity by George Wilmer.

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19
notes: The full physical codex is modeled separately from the work portion and from Fletcher's 1987 printed facsimile.
The catalog title 'English Poems by John Lydgate etc' describes the miscellany and does not establish authorship of every component.

physicalDescription.format: Composite miscellany; mostly 42 lines to a page
notes: The current catalog describes the writing as neat and supplies a detailed quire collation.
The work mapping is fol. 67v for the interpretatio and fols. 68r–97v for the poem; older a/b notation gives 67b–97b.

entities.description: A large parchment codex copied in two phases. The Assembly is in the later c. 1516–1527 addition at fols. 167r–180v, after works by William Cornish and John Skelton and before other Percy-associated material.

holding.notes: The exact work component has record 041-002250006 at fols. 167r–180v.

entities.label: British Library Royal MS 18 D II
notes: Triggs believed B was probably copied from his print C; later Ringler argued that it derives from print D, while Fletcher questioned the exact early-print stemma.
The British Library records full digital coverage, but its legacy manuscript images remained unavailable after the cyber-attack at review time.

physicalDescription.extent: [iii] + i + 212 + [ii] folios; parent catalog also summarizes 213 + 5 folios
physicalDescription.format: Composite codex with multiple textual phases
notes: The Assembly has calligraphic initials highlighted in yellow and red.
The component record explicitly calls the poem anonymous and distinguishes it from Lydgate's Reason and Sensuality.

rights.basis: No reachable item-level image license was available; BL Imaging Services states that supplied images may have copyright and publication permission may be required.
rights.component: British Library manuscript photographs or ordered reproductions
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and applicable copyright jurisdictions

access.entityLabel: Wynkyn de Worde STC 17005 edition (Triggs Text C)

entities.description: An early printed edition conventionally dated Westminster, 1498. Triggs names the British Library copy G.11587 and uses its Lydgate colophon and table of interpretations as title and attribution evidence.

holding.notes: The BL copy is imperfect, wanting A5 (supplied in facsimile) and C4, the final blank. CERL also records Morgan PML 737.2 and an imperfect Huntington copy.

entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde STC 17005 edition (Triggs Text C)

dateStatements.label: current ISTC imprint date
dateStatements.notes: Triggs and Chance, using older bibliography, date this print around 1500.

entities.description: An undated early printed edition currently dated about 1494 by ISTC. It survives at the British Library as C.13.a.21(2), at Huntington as 31533, and in Tokyo fragments.

holding.notes: CERL records a legacy British Library digital object that was not reachable on 2026-07-11.

entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde STC 17007 edition (Triggs Text D)

entities.description: A unique Cambridge University Library early print currently dated about 1499–1500. ISTC says the about-1494 edition served as its copy-text; Francis Jenkinson published a complete photographic facsimile in 1906.

holding.notes: The old AB.4.58 designation belongs to the famous Moore Library volume of black-letter tracts described by Jenkinson.

entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde STC 17006 edition (Chance Text E)

next_steps: Collate the complete Trinity and STC 17006 facsimiles with STC 17005, STC 17007, and Royal 18 D II when those images become available.
Reconcile Ringler's B-from-D and C-first analysis, Fletcher's competing common-ancestor hypothesis, and current ISTC's earlier dating of STC 17007.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact copying sequence among Trinity R.3.19, Royal 18 D II, and Wynkyn de Worde's early editions?

next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services and Imaging Services by shelfmark whether a complete IIIF manifest, downloadable surrogate, microfilm, or paid image set can currently be supplied.
When images return, cache the whole codex rather than only fols. 167r–180v and map the work portion precisely.
openQuestions.question: When will the British Library's recorded complete digital coverage of Royal MS 18 D II be restored, and is an alternate complete surrogate currently obtainable?

next_steps: Ask Trinity's Digital Services Manager for written clarification referencing both the manifest license and Wren Digital Collections policy.
Do not redistribute the cached bundle, and do not use it in a repository designed for commercial downstream publication, without an answer or separate license.
openQuestions.question: Does the exact R.3.19 CC BY-NC 4.0 license authorize noncommercial publication without a separate application, or does Trinity's general publication-permission policy add an enforceable requirement?

next_steps: Request U-M's written interpretation for redistribution of the XML and close derivatives.
Continue to distinguish the public-domain 1895 edition from U-M's OCR, markup, images, and legacy provider terms.
openQuestions.question: How should U-M's current public-domain/NoC-US statement be reconciled with the 2006 XML warning that copyright and other distribution restrictions may apply?

next_steps: Consult William Ringler's 1953 article and ask Bodleian Special Collections to resolve the obsolete 'Douce fragment, f. 51' and former STC 24844a references.
Do not invent a complete shelfmark or model the fragment as a secure facsimile until the physical object is verified.
openQuestions.question: What is the current exact Bodleian shelfmark and digitization status of the Douce binding fragment preserving Assembly lines 1213–1323?

subject.description: Repository work and its linked rights analysis

relations.subjectLabel: General Corpus source XML for acw2946

reviewNotes: The abstract medieval work, two manuscript witnesses, three early printed traditions, Triggs's edition, U-M encoding and page images, modern Chance edition, digital facsimiles, and repository XML are separate entities.
The prose interpretatio occupies Trinity fol. 67v and the poem fols. 68r–97v; the complete-codex cache intentionally includes all 522 canvases rather than cropping to those leaves.

Current ISTC dates STC 17007 about 1494, earlier than STC 17005 (1498), whereas Triggs and Chance retain an older C-before-D sequence; this record preserves the disagreement instead of silently renumbering Triggs's witnesses.

The exact Trinity IIIF manifest applies CC BY-NC 4.0, while Trinity's general Wren Digital Collections policy separately asks researchers to request permission for publication; redistribution should wait for provider clarification.
Negative public-facsimile findings are date-bounded to 2026-07-11 and do not establish that no offline surrogate or future restored service exists.

sourceLinks.accessMethod: Direct PDF from the U.S. Copyright Office

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a transformed local version of University of Michigan CME item ACW2946.0001.001, an uncorrected OCR/TEI Level 1 encoding of Oscar Lovell Triggs's 1895 University of Chicago edition. Triggs took Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19 as his authoritative copytext, collated British Library Royal MS 18 D II, and cited selected readings and bibliographic evidence from early Wynkyn de Worde printings. Trinity R.3.19 is now publicly available as a complete 522-canvas IIIF object; the cached whole-codex bundle maps the interpretatio and poem to fols. 67v–97v, canvases 140–200. Royal 18 D II has full digital coverage recorded by the British Library, but its images remained unavailable on 2026-07-11. Current scholarship treats the poem as anonymous, London c. 1478–1483, and disputes both Triggs's Lydgate attribution and the exact early-print stemma.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Probably c. 1478–1483; first known in Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19 and printed by about 1494
catalogSummary.title: The Assembly of Gods

catalogingSubject.label: The Assembly of Gods
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fifteenth-century Middle English dream-vision allegory conventionally called The Assembly of Gods, together with the short transmitted prose table interpreting twenty-seven mythological names. The record distinguishes this intellectual work from John Lydgate's different poem Reason and Sensuality, the Trinity and British Library witnesses, the early printed editions, Oscar Lovell Triggs's 1895 scholarly edition, Jane Chance's 1999 edition, the University of Michigan OCR/TEI representation, and the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: The Interpretation of the Names of Gods and Goddesses
notes: Royal MS 18 D II omits this list; Triggs reports that early print Text C includes it.
contentParts.summary: A prose list glossing twenty-seven mythological figures from Phebus through Attropos.

contentParts.label: Opening dream frame
contentParts.summary: The narrator's failed attempt to reconcile Reason and Sensuality, his falling asleep, and Morpheus's summons.

contentParts.label: Assembly proper
contentParts.summary: The jurisdictional dispute among Eolus, Diana, and Neptunus and the banquet of the gods.

contentParts.label: Psychomachia of Virtue and Vice
notes: The analytical endpoint falls within a stanza, so no whole-stanza count is asserted.
contentParts.summary: Vice and the deadly sins oppose Virtue, the virtues, liberal arts, Reason, Conscience, and Free Will in the microcosm.

contentParts.label: Concluding dream frame and doctrinal interpretation
notes: The analytical starting point falls within a stanza, so no whole-stanza count is asserted.
contentParts.summary: The dreamer's encounter with Wit and Dame Doctrine, the interpretation of the allegory, awakening, and closing exhortation.

assertion.notes: Chance follows Bradford Y. Fletcher's dating and localization of the Trinity copy context; neither source names an authorial date.

notes: This late date is a principal reason the poem cannot be by Lydgate, who died decades earlier.

assertion.notes: The current Trinity catalog gives only fifteenth/sixteenth centuries for the composite volume; the narrower range is scholarly.

dateStatements.display: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19, conventionally c. 1480 within a manuscript produced c. 1478–1483

assertion.notes: Current CERL/ISTC dates STC 17007 about 1494, while Triggs and Chance retain an older sequence in which Text C (STC 17005, 1498) precedes Text D (STC 17007, then dated c. 1500).

dateStatements.display: By about 1494, in Wynkyn de Worde's STC 17007 edition according to the current ISTC dating
notes: No exact normalized year is supplied because the imprint is undated and the sequence remains bibliographically contested.

extent.description: Repository XML metrics: one twenty-seven-entry prose interpretatio and 301 numbered LG stanza groups containing exactly 2,107 L elements. The five sections are the cataloger's analytical parts below; the XML itself encodes the poem as one DIV1.

formStatements.description: The substantive work is 301 seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas; the brief twenty-seven-entry prose interpretatio is modeled as a separate framing part and does not make the whole work mixed.
A short prose list glossing twenty-seven divine or mythological names.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header's generic code eng describes the scanned volume; the cataloged medieval poem is Middle English.

The Trinity prose interpretatio is retained as a framing part because it travels with the poem in the authoritative manuscript and several early prints; it is not counted among the poem's 2,107 verse lines.

The source XML's 196 PB milestones cover the complete 1895 volume, but only the title page, dedication, interpretatio, and poem are transcribed; most of Triggs's introduction and back matter are represented as omitted-matter containers with page milestones.
All manuscript dates, rights, and image-access facts remain attached to their material or digital layers rather than being promoted to properties of the abstract work.

next_steps: Consult scholarship subsequent to Chance and Fletcher on the Hammond scribe, Trinity anthology booklets, dialect, paper stocks, and literary networks.
Do not revive the Lydgate attribution merely from early witness headings or U-M catalog inheritance.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous Assembly-poet be identified more precisely, or the c. 1478–1483 London localization independently refined?

next_steps: Collate the complete Trinity and early-print facsimiles against Royal 18 D II when its images return.
Reconcile Ringler's B-from-D and C-first argument, Fletcher's competing common-ancestor model, and current ISTC's about-1494 dating of STC 17007.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact descent among Trinity R.3.19, Royal 18 D II, and the early Wynkyn de Worde editions?

next_steps: Consult Ringler's 1953 article and ask Bodleian Special Collections to resolve the obsolete fragment description and former STC 24844a designation.
Add a witness entity only after the exact physical object and current shelfmark are verified.
openQuestions.question: What is the current exact Bodleian shelfmark and access route for the Douce binding fragment preserving lines 1213–1323?

assertion.notes: Chance calls it a London poem on the basis of Fletcher's manuscript study; the text itself does not name its place of composition.
Modern scholarship and the current British Library component description treat the poem as anonymous.
The history of the attribution is confirmed; Lydgate's authorship is not. Chance summarizes the metrical, stylistic, and chronological reasons for rejection.

notes: Triggs defended the attribution and the U-M bibliographic record inherits it, but the cataloged work should not be filed as accepted Lydgate authorship.

scope.description: Text based principally on Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19
Variants from Royal MS 18 D II and selected early-print readings
Later 1999 comparison edition, not the source of the U-M encoding

subjects.label: Conflict and accord between reason and sensuality
Classical mythology interpreted through Christian moral theology
Free will, conscience, sacraments, and salvation

scope.description: Vice's captains and their social catalogue, especially lines 617–714

summaries.value: A troubled dreamer who cannot reconcile Reason and Sensuality is led by Morpheus into a vast allegorical vision. A dispute among pagan gods develops into a psychomachia in which Virtue and Vice contend for the microcosm under Free Will and Conscience; Doctrine finally interprets the spectacle through Christian moral teaching, mortality, sacrament, and salvation.

The transmitted unit begins with a prose interpretation of twenty-seven mythological names, followed by 2,107 verse lines in 301 rhyme-royal stanzas. Chance analyzes the poem as an opening dream frame (lines 1–35), the assembly proper (36–616), the battle of Virtue and Vice (617–1468), and the concluding doctrinal dream frame (1469–2107).

The poem is not Lydgate's distinct Reason and Sensuality. Triggs's historical attribution and c. 1420 date are retained as reception history, while anonymous authorship and the later c. 1478–1483 dating are preferred.

notes: An established editorial title rather than an authorial title; the early witnesses and prints use several different forms.
